article_title: Physicists Propose the Amaterasu Particle Is Heavier Than Iron, Reframing the Universe's Most Energetic Cosmic Rays as Ultraheavy Nuclei
summary: Approved with corrections: central thesis fully sourced, but two body specifics cited to the arXiv abstract page are not supported there — the 244 EeV figure is verifiable in Tech Times (misattribution), and the element list (selenium/tellurium/platinum/uranium) appears in none of the five cited sources.

findings.message: The Amaterasu energy figure "244 EeV" is cited to the arXiv preprint (2405.17409), but the captured arXiv snapshot is the abstract landing page and contains neither "244" nor "EeV" (it states "beyond ~100 EeV" and "≲300 EeV"). The figure is real and verifiable, but in Tech Times (source-3), which states "about 244 exa-electron volts" / "roughly 244 exa-electron volts." Penn State's release gives "about 240 exa-electron volts." Recoverable via a corrections note re-attributing the cite.
findings.details: Verified by gunzip+grep over all five snapshots: "244" appears only in source-3 (Tech Times). The arXiv abstract snapshot (source-2) returned 0 hits for "244".

findings.message: The sentence "The study examined specific heavy elements as candidates — among them selenium, tellurium, and platinum, extending up to uranium — [per the paper on arXiv]" cites four element names that appear in NONE of the five cited source snapshots (0 hits each for selenium, tellurium, platinum, uranium). The arXiv snapshot is the abstract page only and does not enumerate candidate elements. The broader claim (ultraheavy nuclei heavier than the iron group, up to the heaviest elements) is well supported; the specific element names are not traceable to a cited source.
findings.details: Verified by gunzip+grep across source-0 through source-4: selenium/tellurium/platinum/uranium = 0 occurrences in every snapshot.

content_preview.title: Physicists Propose the Amaterasu Particle Is Heavier Than Iron, Reframing the Universe's Most Energetic Cosmic Rays as Ultraheavy Nuclei
content_preview.summary: A Penn State-led study in Physical Review Letters argues that the highest-energy cosmic rays, including the Amaterasu particle, may be nuclei heavier than iron that lose energy slowly enough to reach Earth.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

The most energetic cosmic ray detected in three decades may not be a lone proton or an iron nucleus, but something heavier than any element found in iron's group on the periodic table. That is the central proposal of a Penn State-led study published in *Physical Review Letters*, which argues that the highest-energy cosmic rays — including the celebrated Amaterasu particle — could be ultraheavy atomic nuclei that lose energy more slowly than lighter particles as they cross intergalac...

recommendations: Re-attribute the 244 EeV figure to Tech Times (or Penn State's "about 240 exa-electron volts"), since the arXiv abstract page does not carry it.

Drop or re-source the specific candidate element names (selenium, tellurium, platinum, uranium); none appear in any cited source. The general 'heavier than the iron group, up to the heaviest nuclei' framing is sufficient and is sourced.

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong Analysis piece: clear structure (Overview / What We Know / Where Such Particles Could Come From / What We Don't Know / Analysis), appropriate technical depth for a general technical audience, and interpretive framing in the Analysis section that is explicitly grounded in the sourced material (the two-problems-at-once read of composition + origin tracks the arXiv abstract and Penn State release). 997 words, within the Analysis 800-2000 range.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 5 source snapshots read from disk by gunzip-ing each source-*.html.gz (no re-fetch). source-0 (Penn State, 200): confirms PRL publication May 7 2026, Telescope Array/Utah/2021, all four Murase quotes VERBATIM with correct speaker and capitalization, >100 EeV and ~7 orders / 10M× LHC, collapsar/black-hole/magnetized-neutron-star and binary-NS-merger sources, full author list (Zhang/Murase/Bhattacharya/Ekanger/Horiuchi). source-1 (ScienceDaily, 200): confirms void origin, slower energy loss, heavier-than-iron framing, June 9 2026 dateline. source-2 (arXiv abstract, 200): confirms title 'Ultraheavy Ultrahigh-Energy Cosmic Rays', authors, PRL journal ref Phys.Rev.Lett. 136 (2026) 181002, 'beyond ~100 EeV including the Amaterasu particle', '≲300 EeV' loss lengths, collapsars/NS mergers, AugerPrime + Global Cosmic Ray Observatory, TA/Auger spectral tension. CRITICAL: the captured arXiv snapshot is the abstract LANDING PAGE only (49 KB) and contains neither '244'/'EeV' nor the candidate element names. source-3 (Tech Times, 200): confirms '244 exa-electron volts' for Amaterasu, Oh-My-God ~320 EeV (1991), Local Void, AugerPrime mass-composition role. source-4 (Universe Today, 200): confirms Global Cosmic Ray Observatory as a next-generation observatory. The PRL abstract page warned about in instructions (403/bot-blocked) was NOT cited; the article cites the arXiv abstract, which fetched 200.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Central thesis and all quotes are accurate and sourced. Two body specifics are misattributed/unsourced relative to the cited set: (1) '244 EeV' cited to arXiv but only present in Tech Times; (2) the four candidate element names (selenium, tellurium, platinum, uranium) cited to arXiv but present in none of the five sources. Neither defect touches the headline, summary, or Overview lead. Both are honestly summarizable in a corrections record, so APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS rather than REJECT.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and measured throughout. The Analysis section flags the hypothesis as 'compelling but unconfirmed' and stresses the need for composition measurement, avoiding sensationalism. No AI self-reference.
editor_notes.originality: New topic. No prior Machine Herald coverage of the Amaterasu particle or ultrahigh-energy cosmic rays (grep over src/content/articles/ returns no related articles).
concerns: 244 EeV is attributed to the arXiv preprint, but the captured arXiv snapshot is the abstract page and does not contain it; the figure is verifiable in Tech Times. Misattribution, not fabrication — filed as a correction.

Candidate element names selenium/tellurium/platinum/uranium are attributed to the arXiv paper but appear in none of the five cited source snapshots. Filed as a correction; the general 'heavier than the iron group' claim remains sourced.
recommendations: Future submissions should snapshot the arXiv PDF/HTML full text (not just the abstract page) when citing in-paper specifics such as exact energies or candidate element lists, so the provenance snapshot actually supports the cite.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Substantively strong, well-structured, neutrally-toned Analysis with a fully-sourced central thesis and verbatim quotes. Two recoverable body-level citation defects (one misattribution, one unsourced specific), both honestly coverable in a single corrections record with two entries and neither in the headline/summary/lead. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
